sanskrit: श्री भगवानुवाचपार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते । नहि कल्याणकृत्कश्िचद्दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति ॥
hindiTranslation: श्रीभगवान् बोले  हे पृथानन्दन  उसका न तो इस लोकमें और न परलोकमें ही विनाश होता है क्योंकि हे प्यारे  कल्याणकारी काम करनेवाला कोई भी मनुष्य दुर्गतिको प्राप्त नहीं जाता ॥
englishTranslation: Krishna said: O Partha, there is no destruction for him either here or hereafter. No one who does good ever comes to a bad end, my dear.
essence: Sincere effort toward the good cannot end in ruin.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: A person who does good practice does not lose everything. Even if progress feels interrupted, that effort is never destroyed and never leads to a bad end.
simpleInsight: You can keep going without fear that honest effort will be wasted.
detailedMeaning: Arjuna has asked what happens to someone who starts yoga but falls short. Krishna answers on the battlefield of Kurukshetra: even a failed seeker is not destroyed. A sincere step toward the good cannot end in loss.
modernRelevance: You finally start therapy, meditation, or a hard apology, then stumble and feel you ruined everything. This verse says the effort still counts; one setback does not erase sincere movement.
translationLiteral: श्री भगवानुवाच (the Blessed One said) / पार्थ (O Partha) / नैव (not at all) / इह (here) / न (not) / अमुत्र (in the other world) / विनाशः (destruction) / तस्य (of him) / विद्यते (there is) / नहि (indeed not) / कल्याणकृत् (one who does good) / कश्चित् (anyone) / दुर्गतिम् (bad end) / तात (my dear) / गच्छति (goes)
insight: It shifts the mind from fear of failure to trust in continued effort.

quoteVariants.text: Good action is never wasted. It does not end in ruin.
No good deed ends in ruin.
Nothing good you do is ever lost.
Good work never comes to ruin.
